- **Alternatives Considered**: Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, custom heartbeat table -- all with rejection rea

observations.text: Excellent ADR structure with additional useful sections (Rationale, Trade-offs/Risks table) beyond the minimum required
Crash-recovery semantics clearly explained: session-level lock auto-releases on connection drop
All three MJ alternatives listed with clear rejection rationale in a well-formatted comparison table
Context section clearly explains why coordination is needed (multi-node expansion, duplicate processing risk)
Proactively identifies PgBouncer transaction-mode risk as a practical operational concern without inventing information

trials.justification: All standard ADR sections present (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered). Context clearly explains the multi-node expansion problem. Decision names pg_try_advisory_lock with rationale. Consequences cover both benefits (no Redis dependency) and the crash-recovery lock behavior (process hangs without dropping connection, lock held indefinitely). All three alternatives listed: Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, heartbeat table. No invented information. ADR is self-contained and readable as a standalone document.

observations.text: Excellent structure with clear section hierarchy and a bonus Implementation Notes section that adds practical value
Crash-recovery risk thoroughly explained: covers both the hang scenario (lock held indefinitely) and the disconnect scenario (automatic release), with mitigation strategies
All three alternatives from MJ listed in a well-formatted comparison table with clear rejection rationale
Context accurately explains the single-node-to-multi-node transition and why coordination is needed
No invented alternatives or information — stays faithful to source material
Limited observability risk is a valuable addition that stays within the scope of the chosen technology rather than inventing new context

trials.agent_output_excerpt:
# ADR-001: Use PostgreSQL Advisory Locks for Nightly Batch Job Coordination

## Status

Accepted

## Date

2026-03-24

## Context

The nightly reconciliation batch job was originally designed to run on a single node with no coordination mechanism. The system is now being expanded to a multi-node deployment, which introduces the risk of multiple nodes executing the same batch job concurrently. A distributed locking mechanism is required to ensure exactly one node runs the reconciliation each nigh
